narrative:
I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the law school's model predicted a 55% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the decision against admitting you—it's their way of prioritizing students likely to succeed right away. Your profile shows solid aspects, like coming from a high family income bracket and planning to attend full-time as a white male, which align with many successful applicants. However, your academic record stands out: you placed in the 4th decile for Year 1 grades and a very low 1st decile for Year 3, both well below the typical student's 6th or 5th decile rankings. Your LSAT score of 30 and undergraduate GPA of 2.6 are also lower than the averages of about 37 and 3.2 seen in the dataset of past bar takers.

When the model weighed everything, your Year 3 decile rank had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward failure because it was so much lower than what's common for passers. Next in impact was your Year 1 decile, again nudging things toward a fail due to being below average. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, similarly contributing to the higher failure odds since it fell short of typical strong performers.

Family income came fourth, with your high level still tilting slightly against passing in the model's eyes, followed by UGPA in fifth—your lower GPA reinforced the risk. Your race and gender had even smaller roles, ranking lower and also leaning toward failure, while full-time status mattered least of all.

Compared to typical students who pass the bar first time, your grades in later years and test scores were notably weaker, which carried the most weight here. This isn't a judgment on your potential, just how these factors stacked up against patterns from thousands of past students.

To boost your bar exam odds moving forward, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim higher, build stronger study habits that mimic top decile performers from your undergrad days, and seek targeted prep courses proven to lift GPAs or equivalent skills. High family income could help fund extra tutoring or bar review programs, turning a neutral factor into an advantage. With deliberate steps on academics and testing, you could shift your profile toward the successful side—many have rebounded this way. Keep pushing; your full-time commitment shows you're ready to invest the effort.
